sn12.52:0.1: Near Sāvatthī. There the Blessed One said to the monks:
sn12.52:0.2: “In one who keeps focusing on the allure of clingable phenomena [or: phenomena that offer sustenance = the five aggregates], craving develops.
sn12.52:0.3: From craving as a requisite condition comes clinging/sustenance.
sn12.52:1.1: From clinging/sustenance as a requisite condition comes becoming.
sn12.52:1.2: From becoming as a requisite condition comes birth.
sn12.52:1.3: From birth as a requisite condition, then aging-&-death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair come into play.
sn12.52:1.4: Such is the origin of this entire mass of suffering & stress.
sn12.52:1.5: “Just as if a great mass of fire of ten… twenty… thirty or forty cartloads of timber were burning,
sn12.52:1.6: and into it a man would time & again throw dried grass, dried cow dung, & dried timber,
sn12.52:1.7: so that the great mass of fire—thus nourished, thus sustained—would burn for a long, long time.
sn12.52:2.1: In the same way, in one who keeps focusing on the allure of clingable phenomena, craving develops.
sn12.52:2.2: From craving as a requisite condition comes clinging/sustenance. From clinging/sustenance as a requisite condition comes becoming. From becoming as a requisite condition comes birth. From birth as a requisite condition, then aging-&-death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair come into play.
sn12.52:2.3: Such is the origin of this entire mass of suffering & stress.
sn12.52:2.4: “Now, in one who keeps focusing on the drawbacks of clingable phenomena, craving ceases.
sn12.52:2.5: From the cessation of craving comes the cessation of clinging/sustenance.
sn12.52:2.6: From the cessation of clinging/sustenance comes the cessation of becoming.
sn12.52:3.1: From the cessation of becoming comes the cessation of birth.
sn12.52:3.2: From the cessation of birth, then aging, illness & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn12.52:3.3: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of suffering & stress.
sn12.52:3.4: “Just as if a great mass of fire of ten… twenty… thirty or forty cartloads of timber were burning,
sn12.52:3.5: into which a man simply would not time & again throw dried grass, dried cow dung, or dried timber, so that the great mass of fire—
sn12.52:3.6: its original sustenance being consumed, and no other being offered—would, without nutriment, go out.
sn12.52:4.1: In the same way, in one who keeps focusing on the drawbacks of clingable phenomena, craving ceases.
sn12.52:4.2: From the cessation of craving comes the cessation of clinging/sustenance. From the cessation of clinging/sustenance comes the cessation of becoming. From the cessation of becoming comes the cessation of birth. From the cessation of birth, then aging, illness & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn12.52:4.3: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of suffering & stress.”
